query: effect of apical dose escalation on survival rates in posterior choroidal melanoma patients treated with 106Ru plaques

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Ocular Melanoma
retrieved_docs.content: Vision-sparing treatments in the setting of ocular malignancies were studied extensively with the Collaborative Ocular Melanoma Study (COMS). The COMS “Medium trial” (height 2.5 - 10.0 mm and diameter less than or equal to 16 mm), which randomized patients that received enucleation or episcleral plaque brachytherapy to a dose of 85 Gy with I-125 demonstrated no statistical difference in overall survival (59% vs. 57%) or distant metastasis (17% vs. 21%). [23] There did not appear to be an increase in second malignancies. [23] Five-year local control rates range from 82 to 98%. [24] Other radioisotopes, such as ruthenium-106, are commonly used in Asian and European countries. It is valued for its steeper dose gradient and ability to spare distal eye structure. [25] Retrospective comparisons with I-125 suggest comparable disease-specific survival and overall survival. [26] Contraindications include gross extraocular extension, painful eye, and no light perception.

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Retinoblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Plaque brachytherapy may also be used in retinoblastoma treatment, typically as a secondary option after failure of systemic therapy or other focal therapies. [24] Several vision-sparing focal treatments exist for retinoblastoma. Treatment with I-125 or Ru-106 plaques is acceptable. Tumors are treated to a dose of 40-50 Gy to the tumor apex over 3-5 days. [24] The indications for treatment include residual or recurrent tumors after primary treatment. Tumor size limits the apical height to 10 mm while the base measures 15mm. [24] Salvage plaque brachytherapy has excellent rates of local control ranging from 95 to 100%; however, ocular complications should be anticipated. [27] Plaque brachytherapy offers vision-sparing treatment to patients that would otherwise require enucleation.

retrieved_docs.title: Exudative Retinal Detachment -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Coats Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy, particularly proton beam radiation, is frequently employed in the treatment of malignant uveal melanoma. [42] [174] However, post-radiation therapy, there is an elevated risk of exacerbating retinal detachment, particularly with proton beam radiation. [174] Treatment of choroidal tumors with exudative retinal detachment includes proton beam radiation or brachytherapy using a plaque, transretinal tumor biopsy for prognostication of the disease, and surgical intervention as the last measure with may entail pars plana vitrectomy and drainage of subretinal fluid with endotamponade. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Treatment / Management -- Radiation Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Charged-particle RT is the second most common form of RT utilized. Plaque brachytherapy and charged-particle RT result in similar local control rates. Charged-particle RT is preferred when the melanoma encircles the optic nerve or if the tumor is too large to accommodate the plaque.

retrieved_docs.title: Exudative Retinal Detachment -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Coats Disease
retrieved_docs.content: A variety of treatment options exist for choroidal hemangioma, including laser photocoagulation, low-dose external beam radiation, proton beam irradiation, gamma knife radiosurgery, radiotherapy, cryotherapy, photodynamic therapy with verteporfin, transpupillary thermotherapy, oral β-blockers, or intravitreal anti–VEGF. [175] Patients with metastatic tumors should receive chemotherapy or focal radiation therapy. [176]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Treatment / Management -- Localized Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Plaque brachytherapy involves surgically placing a radioactive plaque, typically iodine-125 or ruthenium-106, over the tumor. This technique is used for medium-sized or recurrent tumors that are resistant to other therapies. One major advantage of this procedure is that it delivers a high radiation dose directly to the tumor while minimizing damage to surrounding tissues. [106]

retrieved_docs.title: Palliative Radiation Therapy for Brain Metastases -- Indications -- Stereotactic Radiosurgery
retrieved_docs.content: Several studies have demonstrated SRS's utility in brain metastasis due to tumors, traditionally considered to be radioresistant such as melanoma and renal cell carcinoma, with comparable local control rates (compared to non-radioresistant histologies). [76] SRS has achieved a steep fall off from the target tissues to surrounding normal structures by the use of multiple converging static or moving beams. [77] This has been able to reduce the risk of damage to the surrounding structures. The dose of radiation is inversely proportional to tumor size. The maximal tolerated doses of radiation derived from the RTOG 9005 study vary from 24 Gy for lesions 20 mm in size, 18 Gy for 21–30 mm, and 15 Gy for 31–40 mm, respectively. [78] The risk of radiation necrosis needs to be weighed against the potentially unproven clinical benefit with reirradiation of a local recurrence with SRS, in those who have received SRS earlier. [79]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Intravitreal Melphalan for Retinoblastoma: A Phase II trial is investigating the safety and efficacy of adding intravitreal melphalan to standard chemotherapy for early treatment of retinoblastoma with vitreous seeds. This approach seeks to improve local tumor control while minimizing systemic toxicity. [132] ( Comprehensive Cancer Information )

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Function -- Plaque and Sheet Applicators
retrieved_docs.content: Plaque brachytherapy is typically utilized in the context of ocular malignancy. These are typically low-dose rate delivery and are sutured to the eye under anesthesia. The two most commonly used plaques are iodine-125 and ruthenium-106. The I-125 plaques consist of a backing made of inert metal such as gold, with I-125 seeds placed onto the wall of the plaque. Ruthenium-106 plaques are made in various sizes, with the Ru-106 electrodeposited onto a concave silver backing. The plaques are usually kept in place for several days, and the patient must remain in relative isolation for that time.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Treatment / Management -- Enucleation
retrieved_docs.content: Enucleation provides no significant survival benefits over plaque brachytherapy in most patients. [14] Surgeons typically reserve enucleation for patients with large tumors, marked extrascleral extension, neovascular glaucoma, extensive retinal detachment, poor visual potential, or for patients who prefer enucleation.

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Treatment / Management -- Photodynamic Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Other treatment protocols are half-dose PDT or reduced fluence PDT, which may have lesser side effects than full fluence PDT. In reduced fluence PDT, light energy of 25 J/cm2 instead of 50 J/cm 2 ) with a full dose of 6 mg/m2 of verteporfin dye. In half-dose PDT, 3mg/m2 dye is used. Although the risk of subretinal bleeding is lesser with these 2 protocols, incomplete resolution of polyps and AVN are reported. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Benign and Malignant Iris Tumors -- Treatment / Management -- Iris melanoma
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of iris melanoma is dictated by the clinical characteristics and behavior of the lesion. Small, well-circumscribed lesions may be closely followed with serial imaging and regular evaluation of intraocular pressure (see Image. Malignant Melanoma of Iris Typical Presentation.) [30] Proton beam therapy or plaque radiotherapy is indicated for extensive tumor seeding or nonresectable tumors; these treatments can result in vision loss. Surgical partial iridectomy may be a treatment option for symptomatic smaller melanomas. Iridotrabeculectomy may be indicated if the melanoma has invaded the trabecular meshwork. Iridocyclectomy is typically reserved for tumors exhibiting mild-to-moderate ciliary body invasion (see Image . Melanotic Malignant Melanoma.) Neoplasms that are large, diffuse, recurrent, or significantly impacting vision may require enucleation. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Treatment Planning -- Treatment Options by Stage
retrieved_docs.content: Groups C and D involve advanced disease with larger tumors and subretinal or vitreous seeding. Systemic chemotherapy, including VCE, is employed to reduce the tumors and manage seeding. IAC, using melphalan as an example, is an option for high-dose localized delivery, while IVC may be required for persistent vitreous seeding. Combination therapies with focal treatments are applied for residual disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Radiation Oncology -- Dose and Dose Constraints
retrieved_docs.content: Doses typically range from 45-54Gy in 1.8-2.0Gy per fraction. [46] [45] It should be noted, however, that doses higher than 45 have not demonstrated a progression-free survival or overall survival benefit. [44] [45] Organs at risk include the lens, lacrimal gland, retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, brain stem, spinal cord, pituitary gland, cochlea, and uninvolved brain tissue. Minimizing the dose to these structures, especially the CNS organs, is critical to reducing the risk of neurocognitive decline and radio necrosis. While specific protocols may vary in acceptable dose constraints, the QUANTEC constraints are acceptable to follow. The brainstem dose goal should be below 54 Gy, which will keep the rate of necrosis below 5%. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of ocular melanoma depends on tumor size and location. The treatment options for patients with uveal melanoma include observation, plaque radiation therapy (RT), particle beam radiotherapy, transpupillary thermotherapy, laser photocoagulation, local surgical resection, and enucleation.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Treatment / Management -- Localized Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Localized therapies address small and medium-sized tumors confined to the eye, with the primary goal of avoiding systemic toxicity. These interventions directly target the tumor to reduce or destroy it while minimizing damage to surrounding tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2643
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure described as “Destruction of localized lesion of retina (eg, macular edema, tumors), one or more sessions; radiation by implantation of source” is classified as a child of the broader category “Destructive procedure on posterior segment of eye.” In turn, the “Destructive procedure on posterior segment of eye” is itself a child of the more general category “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid.” This hierarchical relationship indicates that the specific radiation‑based treatment for localized retinal lesions is a specialized form of posterior‑segment destructive therapy, which falls under the overall concept of destroying retinal and choroidal lesions. Understanding this taxonomy helps clinicians accurately categorize ocular interventions and select appropriate treatment strategies for posterior segment pathologies.

Recognizing this extensive spectrum and its hierarchical organization aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, epidemiologic tracking, and the selection of targeted therapeutic strategies for choroidal and retinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2427
retrieved_docs.content: Destruction of localized lesion of retina (eg, macular edema, tumors), one or more sessions; radiation by implantation of source is a specific destructive procedure on the posterior segment of the eye and is classified as a child (subtype) of posterior‑segment destructive procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1007
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical act of insertion encompasses several distinct procedural methods. One method involves the replacement of the tricuspid valve with cardiopulmonary bypass, indicating a surgical approach to address tricuspid valve disease. Another method described is the destruction of a localized lesion of the retina (e.g., macular edema, tumors) performed over one or more sessions through radiation by implantation of a source; this procedure is classified as a destructive procedure on the posterior segment of the eye and falls under the broader category of destruction of lesion of retina and choroid. A further method of the clinical act of insertion is the insertion by a physician or other qualified health‑care professional of sphenoidal electrodes for electroencephalographic (EEG) recording, which facilitates neurophysiological monitoring. Together, these methods illustrate the diverse applications of the clinical act of insertion across cardiac surgery, ophthalmic radiation therapy, and neurodiagnostic electrode placement, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate techniques for specific clinical objectives.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: radiation implantation to destroy localized retinal lesions such as macular edema or tumors—are a child procedure of the broader “destructive posterior‑segment” category and are performed directly on the retina and choroid. The retina also serves as the finding site for the disease “داء ريفسام”. Together, these relationships demonstrate how functional failure, chronic disease trajectories, and interpretive impairments intersect across cardiovascular, renal, metabolic, gastrointestinal, and neurological systems, and they highlight the multidisciplinary procedural approaches required for managing such complex clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the principal anatomic locus for a broad spectrum of ocular disorders, including hemorrhagic, inflammatory, infectious, degenerative, postoperative, and metastatic conditions. Hemorrhagic involvement is exemplified by choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي). Inflammatory diseases affecting the choroid encompass disseminated choroiditis, disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, focal choroiditis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, familial choroidal lesions (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), choroid‑retinal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), and focal, unspecified choroid‑retinal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد). Degenerative processes include partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, stone‑like choroidal atrophy (تنكس حصوي), general choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), and peri‑optic choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي). Post‑inflammatory sequelae

The retina is also the primary site for retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and various other retinopathies. Therapeutic interventions targeting these lesions comprise destructive procedures on the posterior segment of the eye, categorized under “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid,” with radiation‑based destruction of localized retinal lesions (e.g., macular edema or tumors) as a further sub‑category. The disease داء ريفسام is likewise identified with retinal findings, underscoring the eye’s role in its manifestation. Clinical assessment employs the concept of “general clinical state” to interpret both biologic function failure and uncontrolled disease conditions; uncontrolled disease conditions are associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, highlighting the need for vigilant monitoring. Biologic function failure is linked to hepatic‑renal failure, indicating a critical organ‑system

Vascular proliferative lesions are listed as choroidal‑retinal neovascularization and choroid‑retina neovascular membranes. Serous choroidal detachment (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and metastatic disseminated retinitis AND retinochoroiditis are also included. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea functions as a central locus for inflammatory, degenerative, fibrotic, vascular, traumatic, metabolic, and neoplastic processes, underscoring its critical role in ocular disease classification, prognostication, monitoring, and targeted therapeutic management.

Detachment phenomena are described as serous choroidal detachment, non‑specific choroidal detachment of the eye, and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment. retrieved_docs.content: the general clinical state, is specifically associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, underscoring the clinical importance of recognizing uncontrolled metabolic disease. The concept of “failure (biologic function)” is linked to rheumatic mitral valve failure and hepatic‑renal failure (الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ), illustrating how organ‑specific functional failures influence overall clinical evaluation. Therapeutic ocular interventions for retinal complications of secondary diabetes mellitus involve destructive procedures on the posterior segment of the eye; a specific child procedure—destruction of a localized retinal lesion (e.g., macular edema or tumor) by one or more radiation sessions using source implantation—is categorized under destruction of lesions of the retina and choroid. This hierarchical relationship clarifies the scope of ablative retinal therapies. Additionally, the disease referred to in Arabic as “داء ريفسام” manifests its clinical findings in the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2249
retrieved_docs.content: Disseminated choroiditis, disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, focal choroiditis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, peripheral disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar and post‑traumatic choroid scar are all conditions whose primary anatomic finding site is the choroidea. The choroidea therefore serves as the common substrate for a wide spectrum of inflammatory, infectious, neoplastic, degenerative, traumatic and postoperative lesions of the eye. In addition to these disease entities, the choroidea is reported to exhibit specific pathological findings such as postoperative choroid‑retinal scar (litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief), familial choroidal atrophy (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), choroidal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية), combined choroidal and focal retinal inflammation of unspecified etiology (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد), age‑related choroidal degeneration (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين) and partial choroidal calcification around the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي). Recognizing that all of these diverse clinical entities share the choroidea as their site of manifestation underscores the importance of careful evaluation of the choroidal layer in ophthalmic assessment, guiding diagnosis, monitoring, and targeted therapeutic strategies across a broad range of ocular pathologies.

Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.
